message:
refactor(repair): extract reusable E2E test fixtures and add layout capacity validation

Extract shared test infrastructure from the large daemon E2E test to reduce
duplication and improve test maintainability:

- Add module-level constants for E2E test parameters (group/block counts, seed)
- Add RepairE2eFixture struct consolidating device, groups, source blocks, and hashes
- Extract build_repair_e2e_fixture(), inject_corruption_blocks(), and
  assert_repair_e2e_evidence() helpers from inline test code
- Add Result-returning bootstrap_storage_result() variant for error assertions
- Add test: bootstrap_storage_rejects_symbols_beyond_layout_capacity verifying
  FfsError::RepairFailed on oversized repair symbol sets
- Update beads issue tracker metadata

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-15c","title":"EPIC: Self-Healing Durability (RaptorQ)","description":"# EPIC: Self-Healing Durability (RaptorQ)

## PURPOSE
Implement FrankenFS’s second key innovation: automatic corruption recovery via fountain-coded repair symbols.

## CURRENT REALITY (CODE)
A meaningful portion of the foundation already exists:
- Codec core:
  - `crates/ffs-repair/src/codec.rs` implements deterministic group encode/decode via asupersync RaptorQ (SystematicEncoder + InactivationDecoder) with unit tests.
- Symbol layout:
  - `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs` defines a checksummed on-disk symbol header and group descriptor extension types.
- Scrub engine:
  - `crates/ffs-repair/src/scrub.rs` provides a generic block scrub pipeline that can surface corruption findings.

## WHAT’S STILL MISSING (THE REAL WORK)
1. Durable symbol storage I/O + reserved-space rules (so symbols exist after restart and are not allocated as user data).
2. Recovery orchestration (detect -> load symbols -> decode -> writeback -> verify) + evidence ledger.
3. End-to-end, reproducible corruption injection and recovery proof scripts.

## DESIGN REQUIREMENTS (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
Per AGENTS.md “alien artifact” bar for risky logic:
- Explicit invariants for recovery decisions.
- Evidence ledger (structured records) for every recovery attempt.
- No silent partial repair; fail loudly if redundancy insufficient.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (EPIC)
1. Repair symbols are generated and durably stored per block group (configurable overhead).
2. Controlled corruption triggers recovery attempts.
3. Successful recovery restores exact bytes and is verified.
4. Failures are graceful and explainable (clear error + evidence).
5. E2E script demonstrates bounded corruption + recovery deterministically with logs/artifacts.

## RELATED SPEC SECTIONS
- COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENFS_V1.md §4 (Self-Healing)
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs` (current storage model)

## Success Criteria
`scripts/e2e/ffs_repair_recovery_smoke.sh` passes and produces:
- corruption plan
- before/after checksums

 {"id":"bd-15c.10","title":"Implement eager vs lazy refresh protocol for RaptorQ symbols","description":"# Implement eager vs lazy refresh protocol for RaptorQ symbols

## GOAL
Define and implement the protocol for when RaptorQ repair symbols are regenerated after data blocks change. Two strategies: eager (regenerate on every write) and lazy (regenerate on next scrub or background pass). The system must choose correctly based on workload and corruption risk.

## BACKGROUND
When a data block in a block group is modified, the existing repair symbols become stale — they encode the old data. If corruption occurs before symbols are refreshed, repair will restore the OLD data, not the new data. This is a correctness hazard.

## DESIGN

### Eager Refresh
- Regenerate all repair symbols for a block group whenever any block in the group is modified
- PRO: symbols always current, repair always correct
- CON: write amplification — every data write triggers O(overhead_ratio * group_size) symbol writes
- USE WHEN: low write rate, high corruption risk, metadata groups

### Lazy Refresh
- Mark block group as dirty when any block is modified
- Regenerate symbols during next background scrub pass or explicit refresh
- PRO: amortizes symbol regeneration cost across multiple writes
- CON: window of vulnerability — stale symbols during dirty period
- USE WHEN: high write rate, low corruption risk, data groups

### Hybrid Policy
- Default: lazy for data groups, eager for metadata groups (superblock, GDT, inode tables)
- Configurable: per-group override based on DurabilityAutopilot risk assessment
- If corruption posterior exceeds threshold (e.g., P > 0.01), switch group to eager
- Track dirty duration — if group stays dirty > timeout (e.g., 30s), force refresh

### Dirty Tracking
- Per-block-group dirty bitmap in memory
- Dirty timestamp for staleness tracking
- Integration with write path: set dirty bit on block write
- Integration with scrub daemon: clear dirty bit after refresh

## IMPLEMENTATION

### Data Structures
```rust
enum RefreshPolicy {
    Eager,
    Lazy { max_staleness: Duration },
    Adaptive { risk_threshold: f64 },
}

struct GroupRefreshState {
    dirty: bool,
    dirty_since: Option<Instant>,
    policy: RefreshPolicy,
    last_refresh: Instant,
}
```

### Integration Points
- Write path (ffs-block or ffs-ext4/ffs-btrfs): set dirty bit after block write
- Scrub daemon: refresh stale groups during background pass
- DurabilityAutopilot: adjust per-group policy based on risk
- bd-820 (RaptorQ symbol refresh on dirty group flush): this bead generalizes that concept

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Eager refresh correctly regenerates symbols on every write to affected group
- [ ] Lazy refresh marks groups dirty and refreshes during scrub
- [ ] Hybrid policy applies eager to metadata, lazy to data by default
- [ ] Adaptive policy switches eager/lazy based on corruption posterior
- [ ] Dirty duration timeout forces refresh after configurable period
- [ ] Evidence records logged for every refresh (eager or lazy)
- [ ] Unit tests: eager refresh correctness after single write
- [ ] Unit tests: lazy refresh correctness after multiple writes then scrub
- [ ] Integration test: verify repair works correctly with both policies","notes":"## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS
- TRACE target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, policy, dirty, dirty_since_ms, last_refresh_ms on policy evaluation.
- DEBUG target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, policy, max_staleness_ms, risk_threshold, posterior_mean when adaptive/eager/lazy decisions are computed.
- INFO target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, refresh_mode, symbols_generated, generation_before, generation_after when a refresh is executed.
- WARN target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, dirty_age_ms, max_staleness_ms when stale lazy groups are force-refreshed.
- ERROR target=ffs::repair::refresh: fields group, refresh_mode, error on refresh failure or evidence-write failure.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LavenderHare","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:20:12.974565074Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:21.052867096Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["protocol","repair"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-15c.10","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:20:12.974565074Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.10","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:05.419321319Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.10","depends_on_id":"bd-820","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:37.150397412Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":78,"issue_id":"bd-15c.10","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SECOND-PASS REVIEW: This bead is missing a ## LOGGING REQUIREMENTS section. -{"id":"bd-15c.11","title":"E2E test: survive 5% random block corruption with automatic repair","description":"# E2E test: survive 5% random block corruption with automatic repair

## GOAL
Prove that FrankenFS can survive 5% random block corruption with fully automatic repair, zero data loss, and a complete evidence ledger. This is THE acceptance test for the self-healing durability story.

## TEST SCENARIO

### Setup
1. Create a fresh FrankenFS image (e.g., 256MB ext4 with RaptorQ enabled)
2. Mount via FUSE, write a known dataset (1000 files with deterministic content)
3. Compute BLAKE3 checksums for all files
4. Unmount cleanly (symbols fully refreshed)

### Corruption Injection
5. Randomly corrupt 5% of all data blocks (flip random bits)
   - Use deterministic seed for reproducibility
   - Corruption must span multiple block groups
   - Must corrupt both data and some non-critical metadata blocks

### Repair
6. Remount the filesystem
7. Scrub daemon detects corruption and triggers repair
8. Wait for repair to complete (bounded timeout: 60s for 256MB)

### Verification
9. Read all 1000 files, verify BLAKE3 checksums match original
10. Zero files should be corrupted after repair
11. Evidence ledger must contain:
    - CorruptionDetected records for all corrupted blocks
    - RepairAttempted records for all repair operations
    - RepairSucceeded records for all successful repairs
    - ScrubCycleComplete summary

### Edge Cases to Test
- Corruption concentrated in one block group (stress-tests single group recovery)
- Corruption spread evenly across all groups
- Corruption of repair symbols themselves (secondary corruption)
- Corruption exceeding single-group repair capacity (graceful failure)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] 5% random block corruption: all files recovered, zero data loss
- [ ] Evidence ledger is complete and machine-parseable
- [ ] Test completes in < 120s for 256MB image
- [ ] Test is deterministic (same seed = same result)
- [ ] Test passes in CI (no hardware dependencies)
- [ ] Graceful failure when corruption exceeds repair capacity (> symbols available)","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"QuietSpire","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:20:27.944881986Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-14T17:13:58.941853641Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","repair","test"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-15c.11","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:20:27.944881986Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.11","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:03.092814350Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.11","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:03.835132604Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.11","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:04.586717786Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":113,"issue_id":"bd-15c.11","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-15c.12","title":"E2E test: repair evidence ledger completeness and correctness","description":"# E2E test: repair evidence ledger completeness and correctness

## GOAL
Verify that the evidence ledger is complete, correct, and auditable after any repair operation. Every corruption event must have a corresponding detection record, every repair attempt must be logged, and the ledger must be self-consistent.

## TEST SCENARIOS

### Scenario 1: Single Block Corruption
1. Write known data, corrupt exactly 1 block, trigger scrub
2. Verify ledger contains exactly: 1 CorruptionDetected, 1 RepairAttempted, 1 RepairSucceeded
3. Verify all fields are populated (block_group, block_range, checksums, timing)

### Scenario 2: Multi-Group Corruption
1. Corrupt blocks across 5 different block groups
2. Verify ledger contains records for each group independently
3. Verify ScrubCycleComplete summary matches individual records

### Scenario 3: Repair Failure
1. Corrupt more blocks than repair symbols can handle in one group
2. Verify ledger contains RepairFailed with InsufficientRedundancy outcome
3. Verify no false RepairSucceeded records

### Scenario 4: Policy Decision Logging
1. Run multiple scrub cycles with varying corruption rates
2. Verify PolicyDecision records are emitted when autopilot adjusts overhead
3. Verify posterior parameters in records match expected Bayesian updates

### Ledger Self-Consistency Checks
- All timestamps are monotonically increasing
- Every RepairAttempted has a corresponding RepairSucceeded or RepairFailed
- Every CorruptionDetected has a corresponding RepairAttempted (unless deferred)
- ScrubCycleComplete.blocks_checked >= sum of individual corruption records
- No duplicate records (same block_group + block_range + timestamp)

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] All 4 scenarios pass
- [ ] Ledger self-consistency checks pass for every scenario
- [ ] Records are valid JSONL (one JSON object per line)
- [ ] Records can be parsed by standard JSON tools (jq)
- [ ] Test is deterministic and CI-executable","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:20:41.110041860Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T23:09:20.907498983Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","evidence","repair","test"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-15c.12","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:20:41.110041860Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.12","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.11","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:01:25.776280150Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.12","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:31:02.350539577Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":135,"issue_id":"bd-15c.12","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-15c.2","title":"Implement on-image repair symbol storage (per block group)","description":"# Implement on-image repair symbol storage (per block group)

## GOAL
Persist RaptorQ repair symbols (and any required validation metadata) in a **durable, checksummed on-image format** so recovery can run after restart.

## CURRENT REALITY (CODE)
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs` already defines:
  - `RepairBlockHeader` (per repair block, CRC32C checked)
  - `RepairGroupDescExt` (OTI + repair region metadata, CRC32C checked)
  - constants and deterministic seed helpers
- There is **no** implemented I/O layer that:
  - reserves blocks for repair data
  - writes/reads symbol blocks
  - enforces atomic generation updates

## STORAGE STRATEGY (V1)
Use an **in-image reserved range at the end of each block group**, consistent with the layout described in `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs`.

Key requirement for ext4 images:
- Reserved repair blocks must be marked **allocated** in the ext4 block bitmap (and reflected in free-block counters), so the kernel allocator will never hand them out.

## SCOPE
1. Finalize the exact per-group layout:
   - how many blocks are reserved for:
     - validation digests (optional, but strongly recommended)
     - repair symbols
     - group metadata (`RepairGroupDescExt`) if stored in-band
   - which exact block(s) store `RepairGroupDescExt` and how it is located deterministically.
2. Implement the storage I/O layer (likely new module under `crates/ffs-repair/src/`):
   - `read_group_desc_ext(group) -> RepairGroupDescExt`
   - `write_group_desc_ext(group, new_ext) -> ()` (atomic update rules)
   - `read_repair_symbols(group) -> Vec<(esi, bytes)>`
   - `write_repair_symbols(group, symbols, generation) -> ()`
3. Implement crash-safety for updates:
   - write all new symbol blocks first
   - only then advance the durable generation counter (so torn writes are detectable)
   - on open: choose the latest fully-valid generation
4. Integrate with ext4 metadata accounting (phase-gated but must be planned here):
   - ensure reserved blocks are excluded from allocation (`bd-huh.2`)
   - ensure group/superblock counters remain consistent

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. Unit tests for serialization/parsing (already partially present):
   - roundtrip `RepairBlockHeader` and `RepairGroupDescExt`
   - checksum mismatch detection
2. Integration tests using an in-memory `BlockDevice`:
   - write a full group’s symbol set
   - read it back and verify byte-equality
   - simulate torn write (only half the blocks written) and assert the reader rejects the incomplete generation
3. Logging requirements:
   - tests must log group number, generation, and which block offsets were written/read on failure

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Repair symbol blocks can be written and read back deterministically.
2. Torn/partial updates are detected and do not silently “half-apply”.
3. The storage layer is usable by the recovery pipeline (`bd-15c.3`).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T15:01:32.860960386Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T02:09:14.762904446Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T02:09:14.762789360Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ext4","io","ondisk","repair"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-15c.2","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-12T15:01:32.860960386Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-15c.2","depends_on_id":"bd-15c.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-12T15:01:57.631365276Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-15c.3","title":"Implement corruption recovery pipeline","description":"# Implement corruption recovery pipeline

## GOAL
Wire together:
- corruption detection signals
- symbol retrieval (bd-15c.2)
- RaptorQ decode (`ffs-repair` codec)
- block repair writeback
- post-repair verification

…into an end-to-end, auditable recovery flow.

## CURRENT REALITY (CODE)
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/codec.rs` provides deterministic `encode_group` / `decode_group`.
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/symbol.rs` defines a checksummed symbol format.
- `crates/ffs-repair/src/scrub.rs` provides a generic scrub engine that can surface corruption findings.

What’s missing is the actual **orchestration** and the integration point where reads/scrubs trigger recovery.

## SCOPE
1. Implement a recovery orchestrator (likely new module under `crates/ffs-repair/src/`):
   - determine block group boundaries
   - identify corrupt block indices within the group
   - load repair symbols for the group
   - run `decode_group()` and obtain recovered blocks
   - write recovered bytes back to the device
   - re-validate repaired blocks
2. Define a minimal corruption signal model for V1:
   - Phase A (required for tests): explicit corrupt index list (test-driven)
   - Phase B (recommended): integrate with validation metadata (if bd-15c.2 stores digests) or format-specific validators
3. Evidence ledger (non-negotiable per AGENTS.md):
   - every recovery attempt must emit a structured record with:
     - group, generation
     - corrupt_count
     - symbols_available / symbols_used
     - decoder stats (`DecodeStats`)
     - outcome (recovered / partial / failed + reason)

## INTEGRATION POINTS (CHOOSE ONE; PREFER MINIMAL)
- Option A (preferred): a `RecoveringBlockDevice` wrapper in `ffs-block` that:
  - delegates to an inner `BlockDevice`
  - on read, runs a validator and triggers recovery on mismatch
- Option B: a CLI-driven repair path (`ffs scrub --repair` or similar) that:
  - scrubs blocks
  - repairs findings
  - re-scrubs to prove clean

The bead must pick one and make it testable.

## TESTS (REQUIRED)
1. End-to-end in-memory test:
   - create deterministic group blocks
   - encode + store repair symbols
   - corrupt N blocks (overwrite bytes)
   - run recovery
   - assert the original bytes are restored for all corrupted blocks
2. Failure-mode test:
   - corrupt more blocks than redundancy supports
   - assert recovery fails gracefully (no partial silent writes)
3. Evidence ledger test:
   - assert the ledger record contains all required fields and is parseable JSON

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Recovery succeeds when redundancy is sufficient and restores exact bytes.
2. Recovery fails loudly and safely when redundancy is insufficient.
3. Evidence ledger is produced for every attempt and is actionable.
4.
